summary: I was born and raised in Racine, Wisconsin. Before joining the Navy, I was working in a machine shop there and feeling stuck in a rut. I remember the day we left Racine on a bus to go to boot camp, passing by the very street where my friends were hanging out downtown, drinking beer and having fun while I was heading off to the Navy.

Joining the Navy was a boyhood dream for me, inspired by my grandfather who served in World War I and my father who was a Korea vet. I finally enlisted just after turning eighteen, wanting to escape the dead-end job I had and follow that dream. My entry into the Navy took me through processing in Milwaukee and the AFEE Station before heading to Great Lakes Naval Training Center, and eventually to Vietnam.

My grandfather on my mother's side was a Navy veteran of World War I, and he used to tell me interesting stories about his service when I was a kid. His experiences helped spark my fascination with the Navy from an early age. Though I never met him in the service myself, his legacy influenced my decision to join the Navy later on.

My father was a Navy veteran during the Korean War era, though he never actually went to Korea. He joined the Navy specifically to avoid being sent to Korea, which was his way of staying out of the conflict. His service during that time was part of the military environment I grew up around.

When I was in high school, the Vietnam War was a constant presence on TV with reporters like Walter Cronkite and Dan Rather bringing the war into our living rooms every night. Many of my friends or their older brothers were drafted or joined the military to avoid going to Vietnam. I saw how the war changed people who came back, making them withdrawn or struggling with substance abuse, which made me nervous but also gave me a distant, abstract view of the conflict until I was sent there myself after boot camp.

Walter Cronkite was one of the news anchors who brought the Vietnam War into our homes every night when I was in high school. Watching his reports made the war a regular part of our lives, even though I didn't fully understand it at the time. His coverage was part of what shaped my early impressions of the military and the conflict.

Dan Rather was another prominent news reporter who covered the Vietnam War during my high school years. Like Walter Cronkite, his broadcasts made the war feel like an everyday event on television. Seeing his reports influenced how I viewed the military and the war, even if I didn't grasp the full reality until later.

Before entering the Navy, my friends and I went to Milwaukee where we stayed at the Marc Plaza Hotel on the eighth floor reserved for enlistees. That night was wild, with parties and even a woman who was trying to sleep with every serviceman before boot camp. After getting kicked out of the hotel, we wandered Milwaukee's streets until early morning before heading to the AFEE Station nearby.

The Marc Plaza Hotel in Milwaukee was where my friends and I stayed the night before processing into the military. The entire eighth floor was reserved for people joining various service branches, and it turned into a big party. I remember a woman who was going into the Army and was quite drunk, trying to sleep with as many guys as possible, which eventually got us kicked out of the hotel.

The AFEE Station was just a block or two from the Marc Plaza Hotel where we went after our wild night in Milwaukee. There we filled out forms, underwent medical exams, and went through the infamous physical where we had to stand on footprints facing the wall for the doctor’s exam. It was a memorable and somewhat uncomfortable experience that marked the beginning of my official entry into the Navy.

Ron Lipke was one of the guys I went into the Navy with from Racine. He still lives in Wisconsin, and we all planned to go to Orlando, Florida together on the buddy plan. I haven’t seen him in many years, but we shared that early experience of joining the Navy side by side.

Jerry Jensen was another friend who joined the Navy with me from Racine. He moved out to Seattle and became a policeman in the Bremerton or Seattle area. We haven’t seen each other in many years, but we started our Navy journey together and shared the same hopes and uncertainties.

Seattle is where Jerry Jensen, one of my Navy buddies, ended up living and working as a policeman. Though I haven’t seen him in many years, I know he settled there after our time in the Navy. It’s a place connected to my Navy story through him.

Jerry Jensen, who went into the Navy with me, works as a policeman in the Seattle or Bremerton area. Bremerton is part of the region where he settled after his military service. It’s a place I associate with him and the paths we took after the Navy.

My friends Ron Lipke, Jerry Jensen, and I planned to go to Orlando, Florida on the buddy plan because we thought it would be a wild time since both men and women were locked up for training there. We imagined the freedom on Friday nights would be something special. However, I ended up being sent to Great Lakes instead due to a hepatitis outbreak in Orlando.

I was assigned to Great Lakes Naval Training Center for boot camp after being told Orlando was closed due to a hepatitis outbreak. I initially resisted going there, even joking with the Navy lieutenant that I didn't want to go, but he warned me about the FBI and being labeled a deserter. When I arrived, the training was more serious, with contraband checks and military discipline kicking in, marking the real start of my Navy career.

San Diego was another potential boot camp location I asked about during my Navy processing, but I was told there was a meningitis outbreak there at the time. I had never heard of these outbreaks before, and it influenced my assignment to Great Lakes instead. San Diego was part of the options but ultimately not where I went for training.

During my Navy processing, a lieutenant warned me that if I refused to go to Great Lakes Naval Training Center, he would report me to the FBI as a deserter, which could have serious consequences. That threat convinced me to sign the paperwork and accept my assignment. The FBI was mentioned as the enforcement arm to ensure compliance with military orders.
